sentence: Subjective:
HPI: Kayla Ward, a 14-year-old female, presents with a 2-year history of acne on her face, which is present most days.
The acne is not associated with itching, bleeding, or pain.
She has been using over-the-counter products, including washes and a gel, which have provided some relief.
She has a regular menstrual cycle, which started about 2 years ago.
She plays sports and uses sunscreen with SPF 30 in the summer.
Past medical history: No additional medical history, no family history.
Review of systems: No additional symptoms.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The examination revealed multiple benign moles on the trunk and extremities, scattered skin-colored papules, open and closed comedones, and erythematous papules on the face, primarily on the forehead and central cheeks and chin.
The chest and back were relatively spared, and the remainder of the examination was normal.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has mild to moderate acne, mostly comedonal with a small inflammatory component.
Problem list: Acne; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:

I prescribed a topical therapy regimen, which includes washing the face with a mild cleanser every morning, applying a non-comedogenic moisturizer with sunscreen, SPF 30 or higher, washing the face with the same cleanser at night, and applying adapalene 0.1% cream in a thin layer to the areas where she generally gets acne.
I instructed her to start with a few nights a week and gradually increase to every night.
If the adapalene cream is expensive or not covered by insurance, she can try differin gel over the counter.
I also recommended following with clean and clear bursa gel and a non-comedogenic moisturizer.
She will need to wear sunscreen when outside and may experience dryness or irritation.
Follow-up: I scheduled a follow-up appointment in 6 months to assess her progress.
